hints.concrete: Check tens first using the place value blocks.
hints.strategy: If tens are tied, compare ones.
hints.worked: 32 vs 38: both have 3 tens, so compare ones, and 2 is less than 8.

hints.concrete: Use a place-value chart for hundreds, tens, and ones.
hints.strategy: Compare hundreds first. If tied, compare tens.
hints.worked: Line up digits by place value and compare step by step.

hints.concrete: Use a place-value chart for tenths/hundredths.
hints.strategy: Compare tenths first, then hundredths.
hints.worked: Rewrite with equal decimal places and compare digits.

hints.concrete: Draw fraction strips with equal-sized wholes.
hints.strategy: Find common denominators or compare to benchmark 1/2.
hints.worked: Convert each fraction to a common denominator and compare.

hints.concrete: Partition the whole into equal groups first.
hints.strategy: Find one part, then scale by numerator.
hints.worked: Compute whole ÷ denominator, then multiply by numerator.

hints.concrete: Build rows and columns with counters.
hints.strategy: Use repeated addition from the array.
hints.worked: Count one row, then multiply by number of rows.

hints.concrete: Use counters and take away one at a time.
hints.strategy: Start at the big number and count back.
hints.worked: If it says 9 minus 4, count back four: 8, 7, 6, 5.

hints.concrete: Use a ten-frame or number line to see the jumps.
hints.strategy: Make ten first, then subtract the rest.
hints.worked: 14 minus 6 can be 14 minus 4 equals 10, then minus 2 equals 8.

hints.concrete: Place long rods in Tens and little cubes in Ones.
hints.strategy: Two-digit numbers are tens plus ones.
hints.worked: 47 means 4 tens and 7 ones.

hints.concrete: Think of layers of unit cubes.
hints.strategy: Find area of one layer, then multiply by height.
hints.worked: Volume = length × width × height.

hints.concrete: Point to each dot as you count.
hints.strategy: Start from one corner and count in order.
hints.worked: Count each dot: 1, 2, 3... the answer is the last number you say.

hints.concrete: Use a ten frame to fill in the missing part.
hints.strategy: Think: what number plus the given number equals 10?
hints.worked: If one part is 7, count up from 7 to 10: 8, 9, 10 — that's 3 more.

hints.concrete: Use your fingers or counters to add.
hints.strategy: Count on from the bigger number.
hints.worked: For 2 + 1: start at 2, count 1 more: 3.

hints.concrete: Use a ten frame to show both groups.
hints.strategy: Count on from the bigger number.
hints.worked: For 4 + 3: start at 4, count 5, 6, 7.

hints.concrete: Use a number line to hop forward.
hints.strategy: Make a ten first, then add the rest.

hints.concrete: Use a number bond triangle.
hints.strategy: Think: whole minus known part = missing part.
hints.worked: For 6 + ? = 9: 9 - 6 = 3, so the missing part is 3.

hints.concrete: Use base-ten blocks: tens sticks and ones cubes.
hints.strategy: Add ones first, then tens. Regroup if ones sum is 10 or more.
hints.worked: For 47 + 36: 7 + 6 = 13, write 3 carry 1. 4 + 3 + 1 = 8. Answer: 83.

hints.concrete: Use base-ten blocks. Trade a ten for 10 ones if needed.
hints.strategy: Subtract ones first. If you can't, regroup a ten.
hints.worked: For 72 - 45: Can't do 2 - 5, so regroup: 6 tens 12 ones. 12 - 5 = 7, 6 - 4 = 2. Answer: 27.

hints.concrete: Count each group one by one. Touch each dot as you count.
hints.strategy: Compare: count Group A, then count Group B. Which number is bigger?
hints.worked: Group A has 5. Group B has 3. 5 is more than 3, so Group A has more.

hints.concrete: Trace the edges of the shape with your finger. Count each side.
hints.strategy: Count the sides and corners. Match to the shape name you know.
hints.worked: This shape has 3 sides and 3 corners. That means it is a triangle.

hints.concrete: Use tens rods and ones cubes on a place-value mat.
hints.strategy: Add or subtract the tens first, then the ones.

hints.concrete: Line up the units end to end with no gaps or overlaps.
hints.strategy: Start at one end. Count each unit as you move to the other end.
hints.worked: The pencil stretches across 7 units, so it is 7 units long.

hints.concrete: Use hundreds flats, tens rods, and ones cubes to build the number.
hints.strategy: Read the hundreds digit first, then tens, then ones.
hints.worked: 3 hundreds, 4 tens, 2 ones = 300 + 40 + 2 = 342.

hints.concrete: Trade 10 ones for 1 ten when the ones column adds past 9.
hints.strategy: Add ones first. If more than 9, regroup by carrying a ten.

hints.concrete: Draw circles for groups. Put the same number of dots in each.
hints.strategy: Skip count by the group size. Count the number of groups.
hints.worked: 3 groups of 4: count 4, 8, 12. There are 12 in all.

hints.concrete: For time: look at the short hand for hours, long hand for minutes. For money: sort coins by value.
hints.strategy: Time: read hours first, then count minutes by 5s. Money: start with the largest coins.
hints.worked: The short hand points to 3, long hand to 6. That is 3:30. Three quarters and a dime = 85 cents.

hints.concrete: Look at the bar chart. Each bar shows how many.
hints.strategy: Find the label, then read across to the number axis.
hints.worked: The Dogs bar reaches 5 on the number line, so there are 5 dogs.

hints.concrete: Use counters. Deal them out one at a time into equal groups.
hints.strategy: Think: how many times does the divisor fit into the dividend?
hints.worked: 12 shared among 3: deal out 12 counters into 3 groups. Each group gets 4.

hints.concrete: Fold a strip into equal parts. Shade one part.
hints.strategy: Divide the whole by the denominator to find one part.
hints.worked: 1/4 of 20: divide 20 into 4 equal parts. Each part is 5.

hints.concrete: Draw two fraction strips with the same denominator side by side.
hints.strategy: Same denominator? Compare numerators. Bigger numerator means bigger fraction.
hints.worked: 3/5 vs 2/5: same fifths, 3 is more than 2, so 3/5 > 2/5.

hints.concrete: Cover the rectangle with square tiles. Count every tile.
hints.strategy: Multiply the number of rows by the number of columns.
hints.worked: A 3 by 5 rectangle: 3 rows of 5 = 15 square units.

hints.concrete: Act out the story step by step with counters.
hints.strategy: Do the first operation, then use that result for the second.
hints.worked: Sam has 10, gets 5 more (10+5=15), gives away 3 (15-3=12). Answer: 12.

hints.concrete: Write both numbers in a place-value chart, lining up digits.
hints.strategy: Compare from the leftmost digit. The first difference decides.
hints.worked: 45,200 vs 43,800: both start with 4. Next: 5 > 3, so 45,200 is greater.

hints.concrete: Break the bigger number into tens and ones. Multiply each part.
hints.strategy: Use the area model: tens times the factor plus ones times the factor.

hints.concrete: Subtract easy multiples of the divisor until you reach 0.
hints.strategy: Start big: how many 10s of the divisor fit? Then handle the rest.

hints.concrete: Shade parts on a fraction strip. Count the total shaded parts.
hints.strategy: Same denominator: just add or subtract the numerators.
hints.worked: 2/5 + 1/5 = 3/5. The denominator stays the same.

hints.concrete: Place the protractor at the vertex. Read where the ray crosses.
hints.strategy: A right angle is 90 degrees. Use that as a reference.
hints.worked: The ray crosses at 60 on the protractor, so the angle is 60 degrees.

hints.concrete: Find equivalent fractions with the same denominator first.
hints.strategy: Find the LCD. Rewrite each fraction. Then add or subtract numerators.

hints.concrete: Each X on the line plot is one data point. Count the Xs above each label.
hints.strategy: Find the fraction label on the number line. Count dots above it.
hints.worked: There are 3 Xs above 1/4, so 3 data points measured 1/4.

hints.concrete: Build a table. Fill in each row using the pattern rule.
hints.strategy: Multiply the ratio value by the row number to find each entry.
hints.worked: Pattern 2:3. Row 4 for A: 2 x 4 = 8.
